narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 41.3% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, test scores, and background details like family income and study plans, comparing them to patterns seen in thousands of past students. The most influential factor pushing toward this higher failure risk was your Year 3 grade decile of 1, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your school that year—far below the typical top-half standing of students who went on to pass the bar. While your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 was close to the 3.24 average among successful bar takers, and your LSAT score of 33 showed solid potential, these couldn't fully offset that later academic dip in the model's eyes. On a more positive note, your high family income bracket stood out as a strong supportive factor, aligning with only about 8.5% of bar passers but still pulling the prediction toward success more than most other elements. Your LSAT also contributed favorably, nudging the odds in the direction of passing despite being under the 37.27 average for those who succeeded. Full-time study plans like yours are common among bar passers, who are 93% full-time, so that fit right in without much sway. Overall, your profile stacks up reasonably against admitted students in areas like GPA and background, but the sharp drop in your Year 3 performance was the standout concern that tipped the scales. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many students rebound strongly from early setbacks. To move forward, consider retaking courses or building a stronger senior-year record to boost your decile rankings, perhaps while prepping more intensively for the LSAT to hit that higher average. Gathering letters from professors who can highlight your resilience, especially as someone balancing full-time ambitions, could also strengthen a reapplication. With targeted improvements, your other solid traits position you well for another shot at admission next cycle.
